# Principles of Creative Partnership

**Principle 1: Genius Needs a Partner. Alone, each was great. Together, they were legendary.**

**Principle 2: Constraints Fuel Creativity. The tiny office forced innovation.**

**Principle 3: Give It Away to Build a Market. Shareware was revolutionary.**

**Case: The Weekend Game. Commander Keen was created in a weekend. It launched id Software.**

**Case: The Windowless Office. No windows meant fewer distractions. More focus.**

**Case: The Pizza Boxes. The team slept under their desks. Passion over comfort.**
---

*Generated by [Heardly App](https://www.heard.ly) — turning books into knowledge you can Listen and Execute.*

**Case: The 24/7. The team worked around the clock. Passion was the only motivation needed.**
**Case: The Freedom. No managers. No corporate oversight. Pure creative freedom.**
**Case: The Technology. Carmack's technology pushed the limits of available hardware.**
**Case: The Community. Doom's fan community was unprecedented. Players became evangelists.**
**Case: The Rebellion. id Software was deliberately anti-corporate. They did things their way.**
**Case: The Innovation. Each game pushed technical boundaries further.**
**Case: The Speed Runs. Gamers competed to finish Doom the fastest. Speed running culture began.**

**Case: The LAN Party. Local area network parties became a social phenomenon.**

**Case: The Start-up. id Software was one of the first gaming start-ups. They invented the model.**
